children.name: Wiki Tree Docs
children.description: Multi-knowledge-base skill for AI coding agents with wiki and tree indexes

sections.heading: § Section 0
sections.summary: Section 0 contains introductory placeholder content describing the purpose of the Wiki Tree Docs document as a multi-knowledge-base skill for AI coding agents.

sections.heading: § Section 1
sections.summary: Section 1 is a placeholder section with minimal test content: a heading and a single sentence indicating the section exists to increase file size. No substantive claims, entities, or images.

sections.heading: § Overview
sections.summary: Claude Code is an agentic coding tool by Anthropic: lives in terminal, understands codebase, accelerates coding via natural language — covering routine tasks, code explanation, and git workflows.

sections.heading: Get started
sections.summary: Installation methods: MacOS/Linux curl script (recommended), Homebrew; Windows PowerShell (recommended) or WinGet. npm is deprecated. Run `claude` in project directory after install.

sections.heading: Plugins
sections.summary: Claude Code has a plugin system for extending functionality with custom commands and agents. See plugins/README.md for documentation.

sections.heading: Reporting Bugs
sections.summary: Use the `/bug` command inside Claude Code or file a GitHub issue at github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues.

sections.heading: Connect on Discord
sections.summary: Claude Developers Discord at anthropic.com/discord for community support and feedback.

sections.heading: Data collection, usage, and retention
sections.summary: Claude Code collects usage data (code acceptance/rejections), conversation data, and bug report feedback. Explicitly NOT used for model training. Privacy safeguards include limited data retention and restricted access.

children.name: Visual Studio Code
children.description: Cross-platform code editor by Microsoft with extensible architecture, Dev Containers, and open-source Code - OSS foundation

sections.heading: § Overview
sections.summary: VS Code is a cross-platform code editor developed by Microsoft, built on the open-source Code - OSS repository. Combines simplicity of a code editor with edit-build-debug cycle support, extensible architecture, and integration with existing tools. Released under MIT license for OSS components.

sections.heading: Contributing
sections.summary: Community contributions include bug reports, PRs, documentation, and translations. The project wiki covers how to build from source, development workflow, coding guidelines, and PR submission process.

sections.heading: Feedback
sections.summary: Feedback channels include Stack Overflow, GitHub Discussions, Slack, and Twitter (@code). Feature requests are upvoted on GitHub issues. Community-driven development is a core principle.

sections.heading: Bundled Extensions
sections.summary: VS Code ships with built-in extensions for grammars, snippets, and language features across many languages. Language-support extensions with rich features are labeled with 'language-features' suffix.

sections.heading: Development Container
sections.summary: Dev Containers and GitHub Codespaces are supported. Docker volume recommended for macOS/Windows. Requires minimum 4 CPU cores and 6 GB RAM (8 GB recommended) for a full build.

children.name: Web Frameworks
children.description: React and JavaScript web application frameworks for building full-stack applications

sections.heading: § Overview
sections.summary: Next.js is a React framework by Vercel for building full-stack web applications, extending the latest React features with powerful Rust-based JavaScript tooling for fast builds. Used by large companies worldwide.

sections.heading: Getting Started
sections.summary: Visit the Learn Next.js course to get started, and the Next.js Showcase to see example projects built with the framework.

sections.heading: Documentation
sections.summary: Full documentation is available at nextjs.org/docs, covering all framework features, API routes, and deployment options.

sections.heading: Community
sections.summary: Next.js community is active on GitHub Discussions and Discord. A Code of Conduct applies to all community channels; contributions and good-first-issues are welcome on GitHub.

sections.heading: Contributing
sections.summary: Contributions are welcome; contribution guidelines and a list of 'good first issues' are available on the GitHub repository for newcomers.

sections.heading: Security
sections.summary: Security vulnerabilities should be reported privately via email to responsible.disclosure@vercel.com, not as public GitHub issues. Vercel runs a bug bounty program.

children.name: React
children.description: Foundational JavaScript UI library by Meta for declarative, component-based interfaces

sections.heading: § Overview
sections.summary: React is a JavaScript library for building user interfaces by Meta (Facebook), emphasizing declarative, component-based programming with JSX syntax and gradual adoption.

sections.heading: Declarative
sections.summary: React makes it painless to create interactive UIs: design simple views for each state, and React efficiently updates and renders just the right components when data changes — code is more predictable, easier to understand, and easier to debug.

sections.heading: Component-Based
sections.summary: Build encapsulated components that manage their own state and compose them to make complex UIs. Component logic is written in JavaScript (not templates), so rich data passes easily through the app and state stays out of the DOM.

sections.heading: Learn Once, Write Anywhere
sections.summary: React makes no assumptions about the rest of the technology stack: new features can be developed without rewriting existing code. React can render on the server using Node and power mobile apps using React Native.

sections.heading: Installation
sections.summary: React is designed for gradual adoption: Quick Start for a taste, Add React to Existing Project for incremental use, or Create a New React App for a full JavaScript toolchain.

sections.heading: Documentation
sections.summary: Documentation is divided into Quick Start, Tutorial, Thinking in React, Installation, Describing the UI, Adding Interactivity, Managing State, Advanced Guides, API Reference, Community, and Contributing Guide.

sections.heading: Examples
sections.summary: JSX syntax example: a HelloMessage component renders 'Hello Taylor' into a container. JSX looks like HTML and makes code more readable — it is transpiled to React.createElement calls but feels like writing HTML.

sections.heading: Contributing
sections.summary: React development happens in the open on GitHub. Facebook has adopted a Code of Conduct for contributors. A contributing guide covers the development process, bugfixes, improvements, and building/testing. 'Good first issues' are labeled for newcomers.

children.name: Operating Systems
children.description: Open-source operating systems and low-level systems programming, including kernels and OS-level tooling

sections.heading: § Overview
sections.summary: The Linux kernel is the open-source core of any Linux OS, managing hardware, system resources, and providing fundamental services for all software. Latest versions at kernel.org; documentation built with `make htmldocs` or at kernel.org/doc/html/latest.

sections.heading: Quick Start
sections.summary: Report bugs via Documentation/admin-guide/reporting-issues.rst; get the kernel at kernel.org; build with Documentation/admin-guide/quickly-build-trimmed-linux.rst; join the community at lore.kernel.org.

sections.heading: Essential Documentation
sections.summary: All users should read: building requirements (Documentation/process/changes.rst), Code of Conduct (Documentation/process/code-of-conduct.rst), and license (COPYING). Essential links to kernel.org documentation.

sections.heading: Who Are You?
sections.summary: Nine distinct user personas identified: New Kernel Developer, Academic Researcher, Security Expert, Backport/Maintenance Engineer, System Administrator, Maintainer, Hardware Vendor, Distribution Maintainer, and AI Coding Assistant. Each has dedicated documentation.

sections.heading: New Kernel Developer
sections.summary: Start with Documentation/process/development-process.rst for getting started, Documentation/process/submitting-patches.rst for first patch, Documentation/process/coding-style.rst for coding standards, Documentation/kbuild/index.rst for build system, and Documentation/dev-tools/index.rst for development tools.

sections.heading: Academic Researcher
sections.summary: Researcher guidelines at Documentation/process/researcher-guidelines.rst; key areas include Memory Management (Documentation/mm/index.rst), Scheduler, Networking Stack, Filesystems, RCU (Read-Copy Update), Locking Primitives, and Power Management.

sections.heading: Security Expert
sections.summary: Security documentation at Documentation/security/index.rst; LSM Development (Documentation/security/lsm-development.rst), Self Protection (Documentation/security/self-protection.rst), reporting vulnerabilities (Documentation/process/security-bugs.rst), CVE procedures, and embargoed hardware issues process.

sections.heading: Backport/Maintenance Engineer
sections.summary: Stable Kernel Rules (Documentation/process/stable-kernel-rules.rst), Backporting Guide (Documentation/process/backporting.rst), Applying Patches (Documentation/process/applying-patches.rst), Maintainer Entry Profile, and Git for Maintainers documentation.

sections.heading: System Administrator
sections.summary: Admin Guide (Documentation/admin-guide/index.rst), Kernel Parameters (Documentation/admin-guide/kernel-parameters.rst), Sysctl Tuning (Documentation/admin-guide/sysctl/index.rst), Tracing/Debugging (Documentation/trace/index.rst), Performance Security (Documentation/admin-guide/perf-security.rst), and Hardware Monitoring (Documentation/hwmon/index.rst).

sections.heading: Maintainer
sections.summary: Maintainer Handbook (Documentation/maintainer/index.rst), Pull Requests (Documentation/maintainer/pull-requests.rst), Managing Patches (Documentation/maintainer/modifying-patches.rst), Rebasing and Merging, Development Process (Documentation/process/maintainer-handbooks.rst), Maintainer Entry Profile, and Git Configuration.

sections.heading: Hardware Vendor
sections.summary: Driver API Guide (Documentation/driver-api/index.rst), Driver Model (Documentation/driver-api/driver-model/driver.rst), Device Drivers infrastructure, Bus Types (Documentation/driver-api/driver-model/bus.rst), Device Tree Bindings (Documentation/devicetree/bindings/), Power Management (Documentation/driver-api/pm/index.rst), and DMA API (Documentation/core-api/dma-api.rst).

sections.heading: Distribution Maintainer
sections.summary: Stable Kernel Rules, ABI Documentation (Documentation/ABI/README), Kernel Configuration (Documentation/kbuild/kconfig.rst), Module Signing (Documentation/admin-guide/module-signing.rst), Kernel Parameters, and Tainted Kernels documentation.

sections.heading: AI Coding Assistant
sections.summary: CRITICAL mandate: LLMs and AI-powered tools MUST read Documentation/process/coding-assistants.rst before contributing. This covers essential requirements about licensing, attribution, and the Developer Certificate of Origin (DCO) that all AI tools must comply with — explicitly including Signed-off-by requirements.

sections.heading: Communication and Support
sections.summary: Mailing lists (lore.kernel.org), IRC (#kernelnewbies on irc.oftc.net), Bugzilla (bugzilla.kernel.org), MAINTAINERS file listing subsystem maintainers and mailing lists, and email client configuration guidance (Documentation/process/email-clients.rst).
